review_summary: The transcript is sufficient for concept questions on latent diffusion and why modern pipelines often add GAN-style losses when training the latent autoencoder.

question_bank.question: Why do latent diffusion models perform diffusion in a learned latent space instead of directly on pixels?
choices: Because the latent representation is smaller and cheaper to model than the full pixel space
Because diffusion cannot be applied to pixel data at all
Because text conditioning only works in latent space
Because decoders are unnecessary once the latent space is learned
question_bank.explanation: Compressing images into a smaller latent space reduces the computational burden of denoising. This makes high-quality generation much more practical than running diffusion on full-resolution pixels.

question_bank.question: Why is a GAN-style discriminator often added when training the latent autoencoder used in modern diffusion pipelines?
choices: Because plain VAE-style decoders tend to produce blurry reconstructions
Because a discriminator is required to compute the diffusion loss
Because it removes the need for an encoder-decoder entirely
Because diffusion models can only denoise discriminator features
question_bank.explanation: If the autoencoder reconstructs blurry images, the later diffusion model is limited by that bottleneck. Adding adversarial training improves perceptual sharpness in the decoded outputs.

transcript_quotes: VAE decoders tend to produce blurry outputs
